question: Does an ensemble-disagreement gate concentrate rejection on high-error transitions better than an equal-rate random gate?
baseline: randomly reject exactly one of the same five cases
changedCase: two ensemble sets share a confident bias
checks: spread and rejection are computed before the target is read
random baseline has the same rejection count
all five ensemble cases and five random decisions are present
coverage, precision, and retained error recompute from raw rows
failure: All members agree on two wrong values outside support, so disagreement misses both shared-bias cases and cannot serve as a safety certificate.
decision: The disagreement gate beats this one equal-rate random fixture but covers only one of three high-error cases. Use it alongside support, calibration, and independent constraints.
boundary: The gate is not calibrated as a probability of failure; the five cases and random seed are deterministic teaching fixtures rather than measured model behavior.
